name: Alpine Climbing Performance
description: For mountain athletes preparing for technical or high-altitude alpine objectives. Targets a strong aerobic engine, load-bearing durability, and sufficient strength to move efficiently under a pack in steep terrain over multi-day efforts.

phase_sequence.focus: Build aerobic base (Zone 1–2 volume), establish structural strength (3×5 squat/deadlift/press), introduce daily mobility practice and joint prep. Low intensity, high frequency aerobic work.

Increase aerobic volume and vertical gain. Add pack weight to rucks. Introduce threshold intervals (1–2×/week). Strength transitions from linear load to moderate volume sets. Begin movement complexity on terrain.

Shift toward sport-specific simulation: long rucks with max pack weight, sustained threshold and lactate intervals, reduce strength volume to maintenance. Mental durability work. Sharpening power-to-weight.
Reduce volume 40–50%, maintain intensity. Short aerobic sessions, mobility, movement quality. No new strength stimulus. Full recovery entering objective.

incompatible_with.reason: High-volume barbell strength work creates CNS and recovery interference with the aerobic volume required for alpine prep. Concurrent pursuit degrades both adaptations.

alternatives.condition: event_date is not null and weeks_to_event >= 16

notes: Uphill Athlete's polarized model is the primary driver: 80% Zone 1–2, 20% above aerobic threshold. Strength work from Horsemen/SS provides the structural base for load-bearing. Starrett mobility protocols protect hip, ankle, and thoracic integrity under extended pack loads. Vertical gain and time-on-feet trump gym PRs as the relevant performance metric.

expectations.notes: Weekend long efforts (3–5h hikes or ruck marches) are non-negotiable for alpine prep. Weekday sessions are shorter strength and aerobic work (60–90 min). Secondary sessions (joint prep, foot/ankle work) pair well with primary days.

name: BJJ Competitor
description: For Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu competitors preparing for a tournament or sharpening competitive fitness. Prioritizes mat-time conditioning (isometric endurance, hip explosiveness, grip endurance), complementary strength, and the joint health required to sustain heavy training loads.

phase_sequence.focus: Build aerobic and structural foundation. 3–4 BJJ sessions/week (drilling and positional sparring, no full intensity rounds). Barbell strength 2×/week (squat, deadlift, press). Dedicated mobility 20–30 min daily for hips, shoulders, and neck. Grip endurance work begins (dead hangs, towel pull-ups).

Increase conditioning specificity. Hard rolling 3–4×/week. Introduce anaerobic intervals (Tabata, sprint intervals) 1–2×/week to match match intensity. Strength endurance circuits (KB complexes, bodyweight). Hip explosiveness training (broad jumps, med ball, Litvinov). Grip density work.

Tournament-specific sharpening. Hard rounds at match pace 4–5×/week. Anaerobic intervals at match duration (5–6 min rounds). Drop strength volume to maintenance. Increase mobility and recovery work. Sharpen takedown and guard retention conditioning.
Reduce volume 50%+. Light drilling only, no hard rolling. Full mobility session. Rest, recovery, mental preparation. Weight cut management if applicable.

alternatives.condition: event_date is not null and weeks_to_event >= 12

notes: Mat time is non-transferable; rolling has no substitute (BJJ philosophy). Gym Jones block structure governs off-mat conditioning phases. Kelly Starrett protocols govern shoulder, knee, and neck maintenance — critical given the compression and torque demands of grappling. Marcus Filly RPE autoregulation prevents overtraining when BJJ volume is high. Joint health for shoulders, knees, and neck must be actively maintained throughout; any pain flags redirect training immediately.

expectations.notes: BJJ-specific conditioning requires moderate weekly frequency alongside mat training. Short sessions (45 min) are viable. Secondary mobility sessions are highly recommended given the shoulder, neck, and knee stress of grappling.

name: General Physical Preparedness
description: For athletes who want broad, balanced fitness with no single domain weakness. Targets a rolling program that develops strength, aerobic capacity, work capacity, and movement quality concurrently — the foundation for any future specialization or sport-specific pivot.

phase_sequence.focus: Establish all-domain foundation simultaneously. Linear barbell progression 3×/week (squat, press, deadlift). Zone 2 aerobic 3×/week. KB ballistic technique (swing, clean, snatch). Daily joint prep circuit. No intensity work until movement quality is established.

Increase load, volume, and conditioning density. Transition to 5×5 strength. Add threshold intervals 1×/week. Introduce GPP circuits (sandbag, loaded carries, bodyweight) 2×/week. KB pentathlon volume begins. EMOM density work.
Full deload. All volumes cut 50%. Movement quality focus only. No max efforts. Active recovery, joint prep, light Zone 2. Mental reset.

Second build cycle at higher baseline. Strength moves to wave loading and heavy/light/medium structure. Conditioning increases to mixed modal AMRAPs and named benchmarks. Introduce rucking for durability. Power work via Olympic lifts or KB ballistics.

notes: General GPP is the entry point for most athletes and the default program for those without a specific event or sport. Horsemen's philosophy of elite fitness over a broad base governs selection. CrossFit's mixed modal approach fills conditioning slots. Wildman KB ballistics cover power and conditioning simultaneously. Marcus Filly RPE autoregulation prevents cumulative overtraining in a concurrent program. Rolling structure means no fixed event date — program repeats build→deload→build indefinitely until the athlete pivots to a specialized goal profile.

expectations.notes: Concurrent training across all domains requires moderate sessions across most days. Shorter sessions are viable — the program adapts by dropping lower-priority modalities. Secondary mobility sessions add significant value.

name: Injury Rehab — Return to Sport
description: For athletes recovering from injury, surgery, or significant overuse breakdown. Treats injury as a training phase — not an interruption — with progressive tissue loading under clinical constraints. Targets full return to sport-ready fitness through a structured post-op → rehab → base → build arc.

phase_sequence.focus: Post-operative or acute injury phase. Clinical constraints govern all movement selection. Quad sets, SLR, heel slides, ankle pumps, hamstring sets (knee protocol). Isometrics, face pulls, band pull-aparts (shoulder protocol). Upper or lower body split training around the injured site. Stationary bike, swimming, or anti-gravity treadmill for aerobic maintenance where cleared. No loading of injured tissue.

Progressive tissue loading. Mid-phase rehab: single-leg press (light, progressing), Bulgarian split squats, step-ups, SL deadlifts (knee). Landmine press, eccentric push-ups, TGU, KB rack carry (shoulder). Aerobic base resumes on non-impacted modalities (row, bike, swim). Daily mobility and tissue quality work. RPE-based autoregulation throughout.

Return to general training. Cleared for full movement library on injured site with load restrictions. Reintroduce barbell compound lifts at conservative loads (bodyweight or below). Aerobic base resumes normally. Strength endurance circuits to rebuild work capacity. Weekly reassessment of movement quality.

Sport-specific return. Full movement library available if cleared. Progressive loading on previously injured tissue. Return to sport-specific conditioning. Transition to target goal profile once athlete meets minimum prerequisites for that profile.

notes: Injury is a first-class training phase in this system. Marcus Filly RPE autoregulation is mandatory throughout — no percentage-based loading during rehab phases. Kelly Starrett movement quality principles govern what movements are cleared at each phase. Uphill Athlete aerobic maintenance principles apply to non-injured systems (keep the engine running while the chassis heals). All clinical decisions (clearances, restrictions, progression gates) must be confirmed with treating clinician; this system provides structure but does not replace medical guidance. Injury flags in the constraint layer (e.g. knee_meniscus_post_op, shoulder_impingement) should be set and will filter the exercise library accordingly.

expectations.notes: Rehab phases require consistency over intensity — shorter, more frequent sessions are preferable to long infrequent ones. Secondary sessions (joint prep, movement flow) are especially valuable and can be done in 15–20 min.

name: Max Strength Focus
description: For intermediate-to-advanced athletes whose primary objective is absolute strength development on the barbell lifts. Minimizes conditioning interference while maintaining movement quality and power expression. Suitable for powerlifting prep, strength sport competition, or a dedicated strength block before returning to concurrent training.

phase_sequence.focus: Technical foundation and volume accumulation. 3×5 linear progression on squat, press, deadlift, bench. Establish movement quality and proprioception under load. Moderate accessory work (rows, pull-ups). Minimal conditioning (1×/week easy Zone 2 only, 30 min max). Daily joint prep and mobility.

Volume block. Transition to 5×5 and Heavy/Light/Medium structure. Introduce wave loading. Add power clean and push press for neural drive. Accessory volume increases (weighted pull-ups, dips, Romanian DL). KB ballistics 1×/week for power maintenance. Conditioning remains minimal — 1×/week Zone 2.

Intensity block. Drop volume, increase intensity (85–97% 1RM). Work to near-maximal singles and doubles. EMOM strength waves, wave loading (3-2-1). Conditioning ceases entirely. Prioritize sleep and recovery. PR attempts in final week.
Full deload. Back off to 60–70% for movement pattern rehearsal. No new strength stimulus. Active recovery, joint prep, light movement. Assess PRs. Plan next cycle or pivot to concurrent program.

incompatible_with.reason: High aerobic volume suppresses maximal strength adaptation through molecular interference (AMPK-mTOR pathway conflict) and accumulated fatigue. Cannot optimize both simultaneously.
Alpine prep requires aerobic volume that creates recovery and interference conflict with the heavy barbell training required for max strength development.

notes: Starting Strength governs the base phase: linear progression, compound barbell lifts, SRA cycle. Gym Jones block periodization drives the volume-to-intensity arc. Marcus Filly RPE autoregulation prevents grinding sessions that increase injury risk without stimulus. Conditioning is intentionally minimized — sufficient to maintain cardiac health (1×/week Zone 2) but not enough to create meaningful interference. This profile is intended as a finite block (13 weeks rolling) before the athlete returns to a concurrent or GPP program.

expectations.notes: Strength sessions require adequate warm-up and recovery between sets — 60–75 min is the effective range. More days add volume but require careful fatigue management. Secondary sessions add little value for pure strength goals.

name: SOF Operator / Horsemen Standard
description: For special operations personnel or high-performing tactical athletes targeting the Horsemen GPP standard. Emphasizes repeatable output under fatigue, structural durability, broad work capacity, and strength endurance across all domains.

phase_sequence.focus: Establish aerobic base and structural strength. 3×5 linear barbell progression (squat, deadlift, press). Zone 2 runs 3–4×/week. KB ballistic technique development. Joint prep daily. No max effort yet.

Increase work capacity and strength endurance. Introduce sandbag complexes, loaded carries, EMOM density work. Ruck distances extend. SSST prep begins (KB snatch volume). Strength transitions to 5×5 and power endurance circuits.

Sport/job-specific conditioning. PT test standards (3-mile run, pull-ups, push-ups, SSST). Named Horsemen workouts at test intensity. Strength holds at maintenance volume (2×/week). Mental durability emphasis.
Sustain achieved fitness. Reduced volume, maintained intensity. Alternating recovery weeks (active 65% MHR) with test-intensity sessions. Keep SSST and run standards sharp. Avoid overtraining.

notes: Horsemen GPP is the primary source: durability over peak performance, repeatable output under fatigue. Gym Jones principles govern mental approach and block structure. Crossfit's mixed-modal conditioning fills job-specific slots. KB ballistics (Wildman/RKC) drive SSST prep and power endurance. Horsemen benchmark targets: deadlift 2.5×BW, 3-mile run sub-20:00, SSST 200+ reps (Team Guy Standard), pull-ups 15+.

expectations.notes: Tactical fitness demands high weekly frequency. Sessions under 60 min will drop lower-priority modalities. Secondary sessions pair well with primary conditioning days to maintain joint health under high training loads.

name: Ultra / Expedition Endurance
description: For athletes preparing for ultra-marathons, expedition-style events, or multi-day endurance efforts. Targets a dominant aerobic engine, metabolic efficiency, fat adaptation, structural durability under time-on-feet, and the movement quality to sustain form across extreme duration.

phase_sequence.focus: Maximum aerobic base development. Zone 1–2 only — no intensity work until aerobic threshold improves. Long slow distance 5–6×/week with one very long effort per week (3–5h). Structural strength 2×/week (single-leg focus: SL deadlift, split squat, step-up). Daily mobility, foot/ankle prep, and thoracic work.

Extend long efforts toward race simulation distance. Introduce back-to-back long days (Friday/Saturday) for glycogen depletion adaptation. Add 1×/week threshold intervals. Strength shifts to maintenance (1×/week). Ruck and loaded carry training for expedition goals. Increase total weekly vertical gain.

Race-specific simulation. Long efforts at target race pace. Practice nutrition and pacing strategy. Cutback weeks alternate with simulation efforts. Drop strength entirely. Increase mobility and tissue work. Heat, altitude, or terrain adaptation if relevant to event.

Volume reduction 40–60% over 2 weeks. Maintain workout intensity but shorten duration. Leg freshening priority. Zero strength work. Full joint prep and tissue quality sessions. Sleep, nutrition, race logistics.

incompatible_with.reason: Extreme aerobic volume creates direct interference with maximal strength adaptation. High cortisol environment suppresses hypertrophic and neural strength responses. Concurrent pursuit of both produces suboptimal results in each domain.

alternatives.condition: event_date is not null and weeks_to_event >= 20

notes: Uphill Athlete's polarized model is the primary driver: 80% Zone 1–2, 20% above threshold. Fat adaptation requires sustained low-intensity volume — no junk miles, but also no chronic intensity. CrossFit Endurance principles inform interval structure in the build phase. Starrett protocols address the cumulative tissue stress of ultra training (foot, ankle, hip flexor, IT band). Strength work is purely structural and supportive — single-leg patterns that prevent the muscle imbalances typical in high-mileage athletes. Nutrition strategy (fat adaptation, fueling windows) is a critical parallel layer not captured here.

expectations.notes: 5–6 aerobic sessions per week (60–90 min each, Zone 1–2) plus one weekly long effort (3–5h). Mobility as a secondary session on primary days is strongly recommended to manage cumulative tissue stress.
